title: Drinking Water With Meals May Make You Eat More, Not Less
gist: A new Appetite study of 49 adults finds that drinking water during a meal correlates with eating more food, not less, debunking the common dieting advice that water induces fullness and instead pointing to a 'sensory-specific satiety' mechanism where water resets taste perception.

insights.claim: The common dieting advice that drinking water during a meal fills the stomach and curbs appetite is contradicted by a new study published in the journal Appetite.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A widely believed weight-management tactic may not work as intended, relevant to consumer diet-product claims.
insights.locator: that little nugget of dietary wisdom is false

insights.claim: In a controlled study of 49 healthy adults eating chicken tikka masala or beef chili, every 100 grams (3.4 oz) of water consumed during the meal was associated with eating an additional 39 grams of food, about 49 extra calories.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete dose-response figure linking beverage intake to added caloric consumption.
insights.locator: They found that for every 100 grams

insights.claim: Participants who alternated sips of water and bites of food consumed even more, with each switch between water and food adding a little over 4 grams of extra food.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Suggests the eating pattern, not just total water volume, drives the extra-consumption effect.
insights.locator: Those who alternated sips and bites

insights.claim: Cornell researcher Paige Cunningham argues water doesn't create lasting fullness because it empties from the stomach quickly, and instead may increase eating by lubricating food and preventing a dry mouth that would otherwise curb enjoyment.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Reframes the physiological mechanism at play, useful for evaluating diet-advice claims grounded in 'fullness' logic.
insights.quote: There’s been this widespread advice that if we drink water, it fills us up. But water is emptied quickly from the stomach so it likely doesn’t fill us up for long,

insights.claim: The extra eating linked to drinking water may work through 'sensory-specific satiety,' where repeated bites of the same food become less enjoyable but water in between resets the palate and makes the next bite taste new again.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific sensory mechanism that nutrition researchers or food designers could test or exploit.
insights.locator: switching between water and food could dull

insights.claim: Contrary to the researchers' expectation, people who sipped water faster during the meal ended up eating less overall, not more, suggesting slower sipping heightens the taste contrast between water and food.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: An unresolved, counterintuitive finding that flags the water-intake/food-intake relationship as more complex than a simple volume effect.

insights.claim: The study's author cautions this is a secondary analysis showing associations only, and the team is now running follow-up research to test for causal relationships.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Signals the finding is preliminary and worth revisiting once causal follow-up data is published.
insights.quote: This was a secondary analysis looking at associations. We are following up on this right now so we can make those causal inferences.
